coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI heterojunction photocatalyst to effectively degrade pollutants and antibacterial under LED light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Zhuwei
Wang, Chaobao
Shu, Shuangxiu
Liu, Jiawei
Liu, Juan
Li, Yeping
Huang, Liying
Huang, Lijing
coredata.dc:description: To remove multitudinous pollutants from wastewater, the design of a highly efficient and multifunctional photocatalyst is necessary. A novel CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI photocatalyst (named CSnBI composite) was prepared by combining carbon quantum dots (CQDs), large specific surface area SnO2-x nanocrystals and BiOI nanocrystals to obtain a compact hybrid structure. TEM and Raman techniques confirmed the structure of CSnBI composite. The photocurrent and EIS showed that the photoexcited electron-hole pairs separation efficiency was improved. As anticipated, novel CSnBI photocatalyst can successfully remove tetracycline, methyl orange, E. coli (Escherichia coli) and S. aureus under a LED light due to the hybridization contaction among CQDs, SnO2-x and BiOI. The mechanism showed that the introduction of CQDs promoted visible light absorption and efficient separation of photogenerated carriers of SnO2-x/BiOI heterojunction. The capture experiment and related measurements showed that h+, •O2 − and •OH are active species in the photocatalytic process. This study gave a novel case for facile construction of photocatalysts with tight hybrid structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI
LED light
Photocatalyst
Degradation
Antibacterial

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ESR, Photocurrent and EIS and Analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors genuinely appreciate the financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51805220 ) and Jiangsu University Foundation ( 1291310041 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI composites; (b) Raman spectra of as-prepared SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI-1 composite.

TEM images of (a) SnO2-x, (b) BiOI, (c) SnBI, (d) and (f) CSnBI-1 composite; (g) enlarged TEM from (f), (e) and (h); HRTEM images of CSnBI-1 from (f) and (g); (i) SEM images of CSnBI-1; (j), (k), (l), (m) and (n) EDS elemental mapping images elements: C, Bi, I, O and Sn.
XPS spectra of CSnBI-1 composite. (a) survey; high resolution spectra: (b) Bi 4f, (c) Sn 3d, (d) O 1s, (e) I 3d and (f) C 1s.
(a) UV–vis DRS for SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI catalysts; (b) bandgap of BiOI; (c) bandgap of SnO2-x.
(a) Photocurrent responses; (b) EIS Nyquist plots of SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI composites.
(a) Degradation efficiency on MO; (b) kinetic fit for the degradation of MO; (c) degradation efficiency on TC; (d) kinetic fit for the degradation of TC by SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI composites.

Antibacterial efficiency against (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus; photocatalytic antibacterial efficiency against (c) E. coli and (d) S. aureus at different times. A: SnO2-x, B: BiOI, C: SnBI D: CSnBI-0.5, E: CSnBI-1, F: CSnBI-3, G: no light for CSnBI-1 composite, and H: light but no photocatalyst.
LSCM images of E. coli being treated by CSnBI-1 under LED light: (a) 0 min, (b) 5 min, (c) 10 min, (d) 15 min and (e) 20 min.
SEM images of E. coli being treated by CSnBI-1 under LED light: (a) 0 min, (b) 5 min, (c) 10 min, (d) 15 min and (e) 20 min.
(a) Recycling test on the CSnBI-1 composite for the degradation of TC under LED light irradiation; (b) XRD patterns of the CSnBI-1 composite before and after photocatalytic degradation of TC.
Capture experiments of CSnBI-1 system: (a) antibacterial efficiency on E. coli; (b) degradation efficiency on TC.
A mechanism diagram for CSnBI composites in the photocatalytic degradation and antibacterial process (a) before contact; (b) after contact.
The synthesis process of the CQDs, SnO2-x and CSnBI composites.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI heterojunction photocatalyst to effectively degrade pollutants and antibacterial under LED light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Micro-Nano Optoelectronic and Terahertz Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Micro-Nano Optoelectronic and Terahertz Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Micro-Nano Optoelectronic and Terahertz Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To remove multitudinous pollutants from wastewater, the design of a highly efficient and multifunctional photocatalyst is necessary. A novel CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI photocatalyst (named CSnBI composite) was prepared by combining carbon quantum dots (CQDs), large specific surface area SnO2-x nanocrystals and BiOI nanocrystals to obtain a compact hybrid structure. TEM and Raman techniques confirmed the structure of CSnBI composite. The photocurrent and EIS showed that the photoexcited electron-hole pairs separation efficiency was improved. As anticipated, novel CSnBI photocatalyst can successfully remove tetracycline, methyl orange, E. coli (Escherichia coli) and S. aureus under a LED light due to the hybridization contaction among CQDs, SnO2-x and BiOI. The mechanism showed that the introduction of CQDs promoted visible light absorption and efficient separation of photogenerated carriers of SnO2-x/BiOI heterojunction. The capture experiment and related measurements showed that h+, •O2 − and •OH are active species in the photocatalytic process. This study gave a novel case for facile construction of photocatalysts with tight hybrid structure.

ce:section.ce:para: As a by-product of the rapid development of industry, wastewater contains many harmful bacteria and organic pollutants, which has brought a lot of harm to the world [1,2]. To eliminate these risks, a number of water treatment methods have been developed, such as adsorption [3], ultraviolet irradiation [4], and photocatalysis [5–8]. Among these techniques, photocatalytic technology is considered a long-term and effective solution to water pollution due to its high efficiency, energy-saving, and fewer side effects [9–11]. In general, the emergence of a new photocatalyst with high-efficiency photocatalytic degradation and antibacterial is essential for technological progress.

As an oxygen vacancy metal oxide, n-type SnO2-x has become a potential photocatalyst due to its low toxicity and low price [12,13]. In SnO2-x, the oxygen vacancy has the function of improving its electrical conductivity and shortening the band gap [14], thus showing a good absorption of visible light. However, due to the narrow band gap of SnO2-x and the easy combination of electron-hole pairs, the low photocatalytic activity of SnO2-x inhibits its practical application [15]. Therefore, the design of SnO2-x composite material is an effective way to improve its performance. For example, SnO2-x/GO was applied to photocatalytic degradation [16]. Bi4O5I2/SnO2-x was constructed as antibacterial material [17]. In spite of the photocatalytic activity of these composites is better than the monomers, some problems still exist in the application, such as the loose heterojunction interface, the limited photocatalytic efficiency. So, it is desirable to provide an easy method to construct other new SnO2-x-based composites with higher efficiency.

Bismuth photocatalysts are widely investigated in recent years [18,19]. Among these semiconductors, p-type BiOI, a narrow band gap (∼ 1.8 eV) semiconductor, was attracted wide attention and applied as photocatalytic materials due to its visible light absorption activity [20,21]. However, the rapid recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes is a common drawback for narrow bandgap semiconductors. In that condition, the photogenerated electrons and holes will easily recombine before they reach the surface of the catalyst to take part in the photoexcited process, thus lowing the photocatalytic performance. To prohibit the combination of photoexcited charges and prolong the life of electrons and holes, combining two or more semiconductors to form composite photocatalysts was an effective strategy. Recently, various semiconductors were selected to synthesize a series of effective BiOI series composites, such as MnNb2O6 [21], SnO2 [22], carbon nitride [23], CQDs [24]. Especially, in our previous work, SnO2-x/BiOI has been successfully synthesized and presented an improved photocatalytic performance in degradation and antibacterial [25]. However, despite of the high photocatalytic performance of the composites, the photocatalytic efficiency is limited for practical application and we are still looking for ways to improve the properties of binary materials.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), for the advantage of chemical inert, easy to be prepared, low toxicity, and good absorption of visible light have been received wide attention [26]. It has been applied in various aspects, such as photoluminescence, CO2 reduction [11], medical [27], and photocatalytic reaction [28]. For its π-system structure, CQDs showed photoexcited electron transfer and reservoir properties [29]. So CQDs is suitable to be applied as a suitable modified material for composite photocatalysts. By combining with CQDs, the performance of many conventional semiconductors was improved. To obtain high-performance photocatalysts, the advantages of CQDs drive us to combine CQDs with SnO2-x/BiOI composites to form a novel hybrid material CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI, in which better absorption efficiency in visible light, more effective transferring efficiency of photoexcited charges and enhanced performance will be expected. As far as we know, the construction and investigation of this CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI heterojunction and application in photocatalytic degradation and antibacterial is scarcely reported.

In the study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from the simple hydrothermal treatment of honey. Subsequently, CSnBI composites were synthesized by the co-precipitation of SnO2-x, BiOI and CQDs. XRD TEM, UV–vis, Photocurrent, EIS and series characterization were carried out to detect crystal phase, morphology, light absorption and photoelectrochemical properties. The degradation effect of CSnBI on methyl orange and tetracycline and the inactivating effect of E. coli and S. aureus were studied under LED light irradiation. An antibacterial and degradation mechanism was proposed by using an active substance capture experiment. Finally, this design viewpoint can provide new ideas for fabricating photocatalysts with higher photocatalytic performance based on binary composites.

Honey was produced by Zhoushi Foodstuff Co., Ltd. Guilin, and other reactants were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd. The fabrication procedure of SnO2-x, SnO2-x/BiOI and CSnBI is illustrated in Scheme 1 . CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal route [30]. Detailed steps: 2.0 g honey was dissolved by 8 mL ultrapure water and 2 mL 30% H2O2 in a beaker. The mixed solution was then added into an autoclave and kept at 140 °C for 2 h. SnO2-x was produced as literature reported [31]. Detailed step is as follows: SnCl4·5H2O (0.7011 g) was dispersed in ultrapure water (30 mL) to obtain SnCl4·5H2O solution. Sn powder (0.0592 g) was then dispersed in SnCl4·5H2O solution. The mixture was then added into a 50 mL autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 18 h after stirring vigorously for 5 h. Finally, a yellow SnO2-x wet powder was obtained and washed by distilled water, and dried at 65 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven.

For the preparation of CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI (CSnBI) photocatalyst, first, 0.123 g SnO2-x was mixed with 7 mL ethylene glycol (EG) including 0.17 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O under 20 min ultrasound (suspension A). 0.058 g KI and 2.5 mL (1 mg/mL) CQDs were dissolved into 5 mL distilled water (suspension B). Subsequently, Suspension B was slowly added into suspension Aand stirred for 30 min. Distilled water and ethanol were applied to clean the product, and then the product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h to achieve 1% CSnBI photocatalyst (mass percent of CQDs in CQDs/SnO2-x/BiOI composite is 1%, note as CSnBI-1). By adding CQDs with different mass percentage (0.5% and 3%), CSnBI-0.5 and CSnBI-3 composite materials were obtained. Meanwhile, binary composite SnO2-x/BiOI (mass ratio of SnO2-x and BiOI is 1:1, noted as SnBI) was produced without CQDs.
Detailed characterization and experimental process were provided in supplementary materials.

For SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI, and CSnBI composites, their XRD patterns were shown in Fig. 1a. The SnO2-x pattern contained diffraction peaks of anatase SnO2 (PDF Card No: 21–1250) [31]. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 26.7°, 33.8°, and 52° were assigned to the crystal surfaces of (110) (101) and (211) respectively. For BiOI, the peaks were assigned to the (012), (110), (013), (004), (020), (114), and (112) planes, which were agreement with the structure of BiOI (JCPDS73–2062) [32]. For SnBI and CSnBI composites, the main peaks were assigned to BiOI, which was due to the low intensity of the peaks of SnO2-x. Besides, the peaks of CQDs were not found, which was attributed to the very low percentage CQDs in the CSnBI catalysts. To further investigate the presence of CQDs, Raman spectra were measured (Fig. 1b). For SnO2-x, the typical peaks between 0–1000 cm−1 were accredited for A1g/Eg internal vibration modes, and for BiOI, the typical peaks from 200-600 cm−1 were can be assigned to E1g and B1g vibration modes of the oxygen atoms in BiOI [33,34]. Similarly, the peaks of BiOI and SnO2-x appear in the SnBI composite. Two typical peaks of 1365 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 in the CSnBI-1 composite, correspond to D-band (discorded sp2 carbon) and G-band (conjugated sp2 carbon atoms) in CQDs, respectively [35]. In the meantime, the relatively weak vibrational modes of SnO2-x and BiOI could be observed in the CSnBI-1 composite, confirming the effective introduction of CQDs in CSnBI composites.

To further study the prepared materials, the morphology was detected using TEM. For Fig. 2a, the SnO2-x showed agglomerated nanoparticles and a few dispersed particles (diameter: 3–5 nm). Lamellar BiOI was stacked together to form a loose lamellar structure that could generate multiple light reflections and thus enhanced the trapping capacity of light (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c showed SnO2-x nanoparticles were well attached to BiOI lamellas in SnBI, which indicated that SnO2-x was successfully combined with BiOI. For CSnBI-1, Fig. 2d and f showed BiOI nanosheets were deposited with SnO2-x and CQDs particles. To further investigated the structure of CSnBI-1, the enlarged TEM Fig. 2g (from Fig. 2f) indicated the heterojunction contained SnO2-x and CQDs. HRTEM images in Fig. 2e (from Fig. 2f) indicated the crystal plane (012) of BiOI [36], and HRTEM images in Fig. 2h (from Fig. 2g) indicated the crystal plane (110) of SnO2-x [37]. In addition, from mapping spectra (Fig. 2i, j, k, l, m and n), elements C, Bi, I, O and Sn were further confirmed in the CSnBI-1 composite.

To analyze the elements valence of the SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI-1 catalyst, the XPS spectra were analyzed in Fig. 3 and Fig. S1. Bi, Sn, I, O and C were shown in the full-scan spectra of CSnBI-1 (Fig. 3a). According to the high-resolution Bi 4f spectrum of CSnBI-1 composite (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 158.1 eV and 163.4 eV were ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, corresponding to Bi3+ of BiOI [38,39]. In the spectrum of Sn 3d (Fig. 3c), the typical symmetric peaks of Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 located at 486.1 eV, 486.7 eV, 494.5 eV and 495.1 eV, respectively. The peaks at 486.1 eV and 494.5 eV were indexed to Sn2+ of SnO2-x, and those 486.7 eV and 495.1 eV belonged to the Sn4+ of SnO2-x [40]. Meanwhile, the peaks of O 1s at 529.1 eV, 530.4 eV and 531.9 eV were corresponding to the lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancy and surface hydroxyl groups, respectively [41] (Fig. 3d). As seen in Fig. 3e, the peaks at 618.1 eV and 629.5 eV were corresponding to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 [42]. In Fig. 3f, the C 1s spectrum was made up of the peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.8 eV, corresponding to the CC, CO and sp2 hybridization of carbon, respectively [43]. In addition, compared with SnO2-x, BiOI and SnBI, the Bi4f, I 3d and Sn3d peak positions of CSnBI-1 shifted slightly (Fig. S1 and Table S1). This may be due to the interaction between carbon dots, SnO2-x and BiOI. A weak internal electric field is generated at the interface of the heterojunction. Electrons on Bi4O5I2 and BiOCl can migrate under the action of electrostatic force [44,45]. The result confirmed that CQDs were well combined with SnO2-x and BiOI, which was in agreement with the analysis of XRD, TEM and Raman.

It is generally accepted that absorption ability of light and energy band positions of semiconductors played a vital role on their catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorption ability for BiOI, SnO2-x, SnBI and CSnBI catalysts under UV−vis light was detected. BiOI and SnO2-x presented an absorption edge at 650 nm and 450 nm. However, as the content of CQDs increased, a wider absorbance gradually formed, approximately at 670 nm, which was due to the introduction of CQDs. Therefore, more photoinduced electrons and holes may be generated, which can improve the photocatalytic activity. The baseline curves were further used to estimate the band gap values of materials based on previous literature [46], and the Eg was calculated as 2.04 eV and 2.78 eV for BiOI and SnO2-x, respectively.

Eg is also important for study SnO2-x and BiOI, which were further investigated by the empirical formula: EVB = X − EƟ + 0.5Eg; ECB = EVB − Eg, in which EVB and ECB are the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) position; EƟ is the energy of the free electrons on hydrogen scale (∼4.5 eV) [47]; X is the semiconductor electronegativity, and X values for BiOI and SnO2-x are 5.94 eV and 6.25 eV [48,49]. Thus, combining with the Eg result (Fig. 4b and c), the EVB and ECB of SnO2-x are figured out as 3.14 and 0.36 eV and those of BiOI were 2.46 and 0.42 eV, respectively. In addition, the Eg of the CSnBI composites were also calculated out as 2.20 eV–2.24 eV (Fig. S2), indicating the photocatalysts have good adsorption capacity in visible light.

The specific surface area (SSA) of photocatalysts was studied by nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurement. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of SnBI composite and CSnBI-1 composite was shown in Fig. S3, which all belong to the type IV [18,50]. The SSA of BiOI, SnO2-x, SnBI and CSnBI-1 composite were 12.2 m2/g, 203.1m2/g, 73.4m2/g and 42.5 m2/g. However, the numerical of CSnBI-1 slightly decreased compared with SnBI, which is due to the particle size of CQDs is so small that it enters into the pores of the SnBI composite, reducing the SSA of the hybrid catalyst. Although the SSA of CSnBI-1 has been decreased by introduced CQDs, CSnBI-1 still showed an enhanced activity in following performance analysis. Therefore, SSA played little role on photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ESR, Photocurrent and EIS and Analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The electronic structures of SnO2-x, SnBI and CSnBI-1 after modulation were studied by ESR spectroscopy (Fig. S4) The prepared samples are all single Lorentz lines with a g value of 2.00, corresponding to the unpaired electrons on the aromatic ring carbon atoms in the π-bonded. In addition, the ESR signal intensity of SnBI is stronger than that of SnO2-x, and it is further enhanced after adding CQDs. This may be due to the improved electronic structure of the extended non-local nuclear π-bonded system loaded with CQDs [51]. This extended π-bonded system can effectively increase the yield of photo-generated carriers.

The separation efficiency of photoexcited charges played an important role for the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [52]. Photocurrent and impedance measurements were used to investigate the electronic interaction of SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI, and CSnBI composites. All the photoelectrodes responded quickly to visible light, but the current dropped sharply when the light was turned off (Fig. 5a). The photocurrent of intensity CSnBI composites is much higher than that of SnO2-x, BiOI, and SnBI. The current intensity of optimum sample CSnBI-1 was about 7.32, 5.12, 3.15 times that of SnO2-x, BiOI, and SnBI, which indicated the electron-hole separation efficiency in CSnBI-1 composite was greater than in SnO2-x, BiOI and SnBI binary composite. Besides, after four cycles the photocurrent intensity did not decrease, indicating good illumination stability of the CSnBI-1 composite. The charge transfer ability of the samples was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in Fig. 5b. It is well known that a smaller semicircle in the EIS Nyquist plot means a smaller charge-transfer resistance [18,53]. To better understand electrochemical process, the corresponding equivalent circuit model is depicted in illustration of series resistance (Rs), and charge transfer resistance (Rct) (inset of Fig. 5b) [54]. Compared with SnO2-x, BiOI and SnBI, CSnBI composites have lower Rct, indicating the combining of CQDs and SnBI promote the charge separation and transfer. Among three CSnBI samples, CSnBI-1 has the lowest Rs of 20.93 Ω and Rct of 43.67 Ω (Table S4), suggesting suitable content of CQDs is favorable for the charge separation and transition.

Before degradation reaction, the adsorption activity was performed on the MO and TC for all the catalysts. For SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI, CSnBI-0.5, CSnBI-1 and CSnBI-3 composites, the adsorption efficiencies for MO were 3.8%, 4.6%, 5.5%, 7.1%, 9.1% and 5.5% respectively (Fig. S5a). In the case of TC, the adsorption capacity data was 2.2%, 5.0%, 7.3%, 9.8%, 14.3% and 8.8% in turn (Fig. S5b). The results indicated the adsorption capacity of the catalysts for MO and TC was weak. Furthermore, the average zeta potential of SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI-1 was around 0.38 mV, −1.64 mV, 0.1 mV and −0.81 mV, respectively, indicating that the surface of catalysts was close to neutral. So, the electrostatic adsorption ability of the material to MO and TC is weak, which is in agreement with the result of adsorption experiment.

The photocatalytic activity of the catalysts was detected by removing MO and TC under LED light irradiation. In the presence of pure SnO2-x and BiOI, the decomposition amount of MO is only 55.8% and 69.4%, respectively (Fig. 6a). In contrast, 79.7% MO was removed from the SnBI composite at the same irradiation time. After CQDs loading, the degradation efficiency of CSnBI on MO was significantly improved. The optimum sample CSnBI-1 can remove 91.8% of MO. The results indicated that the degradation efficiency of MO was closely related to CQDs load. The change curve of ln(C/C0) over time (Fig. 6b) was used to fit the degradation rate curve of MO in the CSnBI composite. Obviously, the CSnBI-1 composite shows the supreme apparent reaction rate constant k value (0.0374 min−1), which is 2.0, 3.56 and 1.47 times as that of pure BiOI (0.0184 min−1), SnO2-x (0.0105 min−1) and SnBI (0.0254 min−1), respectively.

The degradation curves of TC by SnO2-x, BiOI, SnBI and CSnBI-1 are estimated (Fig. 6c). The degradation efficiency of optimum CSnBI-1 composite reached 78.9% within 75 min, which was significantly higher than single SnO2-x (9.9%), BiOI (65.9%) and SnBI (70.2%) under the same conditions. The change curve of ln (C/C0) over time (Fig. 6d) was used to fit the degradation rate constant of TC by CSnBI-1 composite, which was 0.0222 min−1, 18.5, 3.3 and 1.3 times of SnO2-x (0.0012 min−1), times of BiOI (0.0068 min−1) and SnBI (0.0166 min−1), respectively. Along with the degradation experiment, the absorbance of MO (Fig. S6a) and TC (Fig. S6b) solutions decreased sharply or even disappeared according to the liquid UV absorption spectra. Furthermore, the mineralization degree of TC was analyzed by total organic carbon (TOC). After 75 min of photocatalytic reaction, the TOC removal rate reached 54.7%, which indicated that CSnBI-1 composite had a good ability to degrade TC (Table S2).

E. coli and S. aureus were chosen as experimental objects, the photo-bactericidal activity of the synthesized catalysts was studied. As seen in Fig. 7a and b, light alone or catalyst alone had little effect on the bacteria, which indicated the material is non-toxic and the light is harmless to bacteria. The bactericidal effect of CSnBI on E. coli and S. aureus is superior to other photocatalysts under LED light. After being irradiated 20 min for E. coli and S. aureus, the bactericidal efficiency of CSnBI-1 was 100% and 99.9%, while those of SnO2-x and BiOI were only about 28% and 31.7% for E. coli, 28.8% and 32.5% for S. aureus, respectively, respectively. Importantly, the bactericidal efficiency of CSnBI-0.5 and CSnBI-3 is lower than that of CSnBI-1. The reason may be that the introduction of 1% CQDs in the composite material was not only beneficial for the semiconductor to promote the absorption of LED light but also reduced the combination of photo-induced charge inhibition. Furthermore, the antibacterial plate confirmed the antibacterial effect of the material on bacteria within 20 min and CSnBI-1 is the optimum sample (Fig. 7c and d). From the above analysis, the suitable content of CQDs (1%) may improve the efficiency of electron transfer and absorption of visible light. As the carbon content increases, the performance of CSnBI-3 decreases, the reason may be due to CQDs covering the surface of the composite material and inhibiting the visible light utilization of the composite.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CSnBI composites was compared with related kind of literatures (Table S3). Compared with SnBI and related composites, it was found that CSnBI required lower dose for the degradation of TC and shorter time for the antibacterial in photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, BiOI composites can not only remove MO and TC, but also destroy E. coli and S. aureus. The result was interesting for water treatment.

The apoptosis process of bacteria was detected by fluorescence microscope and scanning electron microscope. Fluorescence microscopy results show different colors (red or green) because of the difference in binding ability between the DNA of living/dead bacteria and different dyes. The living bacteria fluoresce was green while dead bacteria with damaged cell membranes emitted red fluorescence. Initially, untreated cells emitted a strong green signal (Fig. 8a), while the proportion of red fluorescence increased with the extension of the reaction time (Fig. 8b–d). Finally, all fluorescent signals turned red at 20 min (Fig. 8e). This indicated that the integrity of the bacterial membrane is affected by radiation and eventually the bacteria were destroyed during the photocatalytic process, which was consistent with plate diffusion test. The membrane rupture process was observed in more detail by SEM. As shown in Fig. 9a, bacteria were rod-shaped cells with smooth surfaces and were tightly bound with materials. However, after 20 min of light, the cell surface wrinkleed and the cavity almost intersected the cell envelope (Fig. 9e). The results showed that light-generated reactive species in this system could rapidly inactivate E. coli, which may be the result of n-p heterojunctions and good dispersive of CQDs.

The repeatability of TC degradation by the best sample CSnBI-1 composite was further studied, and the XRD patterns before and after the photocatalytic experiment were analyzed (Fig. 10 ). After four cycles of degradation, the CSnBI-1 still showed high degradation activity, indicating that the composite could be repeatedly used for TC degradation (Fig. 10a). The crystal structure of CSnBI-1 did not change significantly before and after the cycling degradation (Fig. 10b). The above studies show that the CSnBI composite is stable.

The role of active substances in photocatalytic reaction was studied by introducing the trapping agent into the reaction solution under LED light (Fig. 11a). Tertiary butanol (TPA), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), benzoquinone (BQ) and Cr6+ were utilized to detect hydroxyl radical (•OH), photogenerated hole (h+), superoxide radical species (•O2 −) and electrons (e), respectively. For pure SnO2-x, BiOI and SnBI, after the addition of NaC2O4, the TC degradation efficiency showed an obvious decrease. After adding BQ and TBA, the degradation efficiency decreased slightly, suggesting that h+ played an important role and •OH and •O2 − played a minor role (Fig. S7a, S7b and S7c). For CSnBI-1 in photocatalytic antibacterial of E. coli (Fig. 11a) and TC degradation (Fig. 11b), after the addition of Na2C2O4, BQ and TBA, the photocatalytic efficiency decreased obviously, indicating h+, •OH and •O2 − all played a certain role, and h+ played an important role. In the meantime, the adding of Cr6+ decreased slightly on the antibacterial efficiency of E. coli, showing that e− played a slight role (Fig. 11a). Compare with that of SnBI, h+, •OH and •O2 − are still active species in CSnBI-1 system and h+ is main reactive species, indicating the combining of CQDs did not change the active species.

According to the potentials as well as the main active species in the reaction process, a potential photocatalytic mechanism for the antibacterial and degradation of antibiotics by the CSnBI-1 n-p heterojunction is schematically proposed (Fig. 12 ). To understand the mechanism of CSnBI photocatalyst, Mott-Schottky measurement is taken to determine structure type (Fig. S8). It can be observed that the slopes of BiOI and SnO2-x are negative and positive, respectively, indicating p-type BiOI and n-type SnO2-x semiconductors in this system. The flat band locations of SnO2-x and BiOI were − 0.17 V and 2.20 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), which were further converted to 0.02 V and 2.39 V (vs. NHE) [55]. For n-type SnO2-x and p-type BiOI, their Ef locations are near the CB of SnO2-x and the VB of BiOI, respectively (Fig. 12a). When n-type semiconductor and p-type semiconductor are separated, their Fermi energy level (Ef) is the chemical potential of thermodynamic equilibrium. While two semiconductors come into contact, the CB of BiOI shifts to a higher position, and the VB of SnO2-x shifts to a lower position, until the Fermi levels (Ef) changed to reach equilibration [20,25] (Fig. 12b). In this way, the EVB of SnO2-x is higher than H2O/•OH (+2.27 eV) [56], the h+ can combine with H2O to form •OH. The ECB of BiOI is lower than the redox potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV) [57], so the e− will interact with O2 molecules to create •O2 −. In the meantime, due to the excellent electron conductivity and good dispensability of CQDs, their positions may exist in three situations. (1) CQDs attach to BiOI; (2) CQDs attach to SnO2-x; (3) CQDs exist between SnO2-x and BiOI. Thus, there are three possible pathways to transfer photogenerated electrons in the CSnBI-1 composite. First, the electrons on the BiOI moved directly to the CB of SnO2-x; second, electrons transferred via the vectorial transfer pathway (CBBiOI → CQDs → CBSnO2-x); finally, the e− on the CB of SnO2-x and BiOI transferred to the environment by CQDs, reducing the combination of electrons and holes.

Novel CSnBI photocatalysts were constructed by in situ synthesis, in which CQDs acted as electron shuttle bridges as predicted. TEM, XRD, XPS and Raman analyses revealed the coexistence of Bi, O, C, Sn, I in CSnBI, which was fabricated by the flake-like structure of BiOI and the nanoparticles of SnO2-x and CQDs. The CSnBI demonstrated better TC degradation efficiency than pure BiOI, SnO2-x and SnBI, and CSnBI-1 could inactivate E. coli and S. aureus in 20 min. The implementation of CQDs increased the performance compared with SnBI, which was presumably due to the efficient separation and transition of photoexcited charges in the CSnBI composite. Because of the excellent degradation ability and excellent antibacterial ability of CSnBI composites, this study give a new insight for promoting the photocatalytic efficiency of existing binary photocatalysts.

Zhuwei Song: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Chaobao Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shuangxiu Shu: Software, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jiawei Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Juan Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Yeping Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Formal analysis. Liying Huang: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Lijing Huang: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors genuinely appreciate the financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51805220) and Jiangsu University Foundation (1291310041).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Constructing an S-scheme CuBi2O4/Bi4O5I2 heterojunction for light emitting diode-driven pollutant degradation and bacterial inactivation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text:
J. Liu, L. Huang, Y. Li, J. Yao, S. Shu, L. Huang, Y. Song, Q. Tian,

Constructing an S-scheme CuBi(2)O4/Bi4O5I2 heterojunction for light emitting diode-driven pollutant degradation and bacterial inactivation

, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 621 (2022) 295-310.
